answer:
Yes — there are results that push against the simple advice “pick the most interesting job possible and you’ll be much happier.” But the evidence does **not** say interesting work is irrelevant. A fair summary is:

> Interest/fit matters, but it is usually only one moderate predictor of job satisfaction. Day-to-day autonomy, coworkers, manager quality, workload, meaning, growth, and role clarity often matter as much or more. Also, people adapt to “coolness” surprisingly fast.

### What the research says

#### 1. “Interest fit” predicts satisfaction, but modestly
A large vocational-psychology literature studies whether people are happier when their job matches their interests — e.g. Holland/RIASEC “person–environment congruence.”

Meta-analyses generally find positive but not huge correlations between interest congruence and job satisfaction, often around **r ≈ .15–.25** depending on measures and samples. That means it is real, but it explains only a modest fraction of the variance.

Examples: Assouline & Meir 1987; Tranberg, Slane & Ekeberg 1993; Spokane, Meir & Catalano 2000; later work by Nye and colleagues.

Interpretation: if Anthropic is much more intellectually interesting, that is a real factor. But the literature does not support “maximize interestingness above everything else.”

#### 2. Broader person-job fit and work design matter more
When researchers look beyond “interest” to broader fit — skill use, autonomy, values, role demands, social environment — the relationships with job satisfaction are stronger.

Meta-analyses on person-job fit and work design find that things like:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task significance,
- feedback,
- social support,
- manageable demands,
- good supervision,
- fairness,

are major predictors of job satisfaction and motivation. See e.g. Kristof-Brown et al. 2005 on fit, and Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007 on work design.

So “interesting technical content” is only one part of “will this job feel good to live inside?”

#### 3. Job changes often have a honeymoon effect
There is evidence for a “honeymoon-hangover” pattern after job changes: people often feel a boost after moving, then satisfaction partially regresses as novelty fades and the new job’s annoyances become salient. See Boswell et al. 2005 and related panel-data work.

This is relevant because a Google offer may feel exciting partly because it is new, higher-status, and gives you the thing you wanted — leading a team. But Anthropic’s interestingness may also be partly subject to adaptation. The question is less “which sounds exciting now?” and more “which average Tuesday would I rather have for 18 months?”

#### 4. Job satisfaction affects life satisfaction, but not one-for-one
Job satisfaction and overall life satisfaction are correlated, often around **r ≈ .3–.4** in meta-analytic work, but they are not the same thing. Personality, relationships, health, location, stress, commute, sleep, and financial security also matter.

So even a meaningful difference in job interestingness may translate into a smaller difference in overall happiness than expected.

### Applying this to your case

I would not frame it as:

> Anthropic = interesting, Google = salary/management.

Instead ask:

> Which job gives me more of the psychological nutrients I actually want?

For you, the Google role may be “less interesting” in the technical/research sense, but team leadership could be a different kind of interesting: mentoring, setting direction, having leverage, building taste in people, owning outcomes. If you have long wanted to lead, that may be a genuine intrinsic motive, not merely a résumé/status motive.

On the other hand, if the Google work is genuinely dull to you and the main positives are “slightly higher pay” plus “manager title,” I’d be cautious. At your likely compensation level, a modest salary increase probably will not move happiness much unless it changes your financial trajectory meaningfully. And managing a team on uninteresting work can become a grind.

### A practical decision test

Imagine ten normal Tuesdays in each role.

For each, estimate:

- How much of the day is energizing?
- How much agency do I have?
- Do I respect and enjoy the people?
- Am I learning something I care about?
- Will I be proud of the work?
- Does the role build the career capital I want?
- How bad are the recurring annoyances?
- If nothing changed for two years, would I still be glad?

Then separate “peak narrative” from “daily texture”:

- Peak narrative: “I work on frontier AI at Anthropic” vs “I lead a team at Google.”
- Daily texture: meetings, code reviews, research discussions, roadmaps, evals, hiring, stakeholder management, perf, politics, product constraints.

Research suggests daily texture matters a lot.

### My tentative read

If the Google role is only **a bit** higher salary and **much** less interesting, I would not move just because people say management experience is valuable. But if leading a small team is itself one of the main things you want to explore, and the team/manager/scope at Google look genuinely good, it could be rational even with less exciting technical content.

A good next move might be to ask Anthropic explicitly whether you can get a team-lead or project-lead path there. If you can get “interesting work + leadership growth” without leaving, that likely dominates. If Anthropic cannot offer that and leadership is a deep goal, Google becomes more compelling.

The evidence-based answer is: interesting work matters, but “most interesting possible job” is probably not the happiness-maximizing criterion. Optimize for a bundle: meaningful-enough work, autonomy, growth, people, stress level, and the identity/career path you actually want.

answer:
Yes — the closest research literature says: **interestingness / interest-fit matters, but it is not remotely the only thing that matters, and the measured relationship with happiness is usually modest.** The evidence does *not* support “always pick the most interesting job possible” as a general rule.

A useful way to parse it:

## 1. “Interest fit” predicts job satisfaction, but only moderately

The relevant field is vocational psychology / person–environment fit, often using Holland/RIASEC-style “do your interests match the work?” measures.

Meta-analyses generally find that **interest congruence is positively related to job satisfaction**, but the correlations are often in the rough range of **r ≈ .10–.30** depending on measure and study.

Examples:

- **Assouline & Meir 1987**, meta-analysis of person–environment congruence: found a positive but not huge relationship between interest congruence and well-being/satisfaction.
- **Spokane, Meir & Catalano 2000**, review of Holland congruence research: concluded congruence matters, but effects are modest and measurement-sensitive.
- **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow 2012**, quantitative review of vocational interests: interests predict satisfaction/performance/retention, but not at a level suggesting “maximize interest and ignore everything else.”

Interpretation: matching your intellectual interests to the job helps, but it may explain only a few percent of the variance in satisfaction in many datasets. That is meaningful, but not decisive.

## 2. Broader job quality variables often matter as much or more

Work design research finds that things like:

- autonomy,
- social support,
- quality of manager,
- role clarity,
- manageable workload,
- feedback,
- task significance,
- skill variety,
- low chronic stress,

are strongly related to job satisfaction.

For example, **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007** meta-analyzed work-design features and found quite substantial relationships between job characteristics and satisfaction. Social support, autonomy, feedback, task significance, etc. often show correlations comparable to or larger than narrow “interest match.”

This matters for your case because “less interesting but leading a small team” may come with more:

- autonomy,
- ownership,
- mentorship,
- status,
- skill growth,
- influence,
- career optionality,

even if the technical/research content is less intrinsically fascinating.

## 3. Job satisfaction is related to life satisfaction, but not identical

Meta-analyses find that **job satisfaction and overall subjective well-being are correlated**, often around **r ≈ .3–.5**, but the causal direction is bidirectional: happier people like their jobs more, and better jobs make people happier.

So even if interest-fit improves job satisfaction, the downstream effect on total life happiness can be diluted by everything else: relationships, health, sleep, commute, stress, family, identity, money, etc.

A rough intuition:

> Interest fit → moderate effect on job satisfaction → smaller/uncertain effect on total life satisfaction.

So if someone says “pick the most interesting job, that’s what will make you happy,” the research does not strongly support that as a universal rule.

## 4. People may over-focus on the salient abstract difference

There is also affective-forecasting research suggesting people often overestimate how much one prominent feature of a choice will affect day-to-day happiness. In job decisions, “this topic is fascinating” or “this company is prestigious” can dominate attention, while the lived experience may be more affected by meetings, colleagues, manager quality, autonomy, pace, stress, and whether the work gives you energy.

Kahneman et al.’s day-reconstruction work is relevant here: experienced well-being is very tied to ordinary daily episodes, not just global judgments about how meaningful or impressive one’s job is.

## 5. But “interesting” may be a proxy for deeper things

Important caveat: when people say “interesting work,” they may actually mean several different things:

- intellectual stimulation,
- mission alignment,
- sense of importance,
- identity fit,
- being around excellent colleagues,
- learning rate,
- flow,
- status among peers,
- future career capital.

Those are not all the same. If your current Anthropic work is not merely “interesting” but also deeply aligned with your values, unusually high-learning, and surrounded by people you admire, then the average research finding may understate its importance for you.

Conversely, if Google is “less interesting” only in topic but gives you the leadership path you have wanted for years, that may itself be intrinsically interesting in a different dimension.

## My practical takeaway

The literature supports something like:

> Don’t maximize “interestingness” alone. Use it as a constraint, then optimize for autonomy, people, growth, stress, meaning, and future options.

For your specific decision, I would ask:

1. **Is the Google role below your minimum-interest threshold, or merely less fascinating than Anthropic?**  
   If below threshold, danger. If merely less fascinating, other factors may compensate.

2. **Do you genuinely want management, or do you like the idea/status of management?**  
   Leading a small team can be rewarding, but it often means less deep technical work.

3. **Can Anthropic offer a path to team leadership?**  
   If your main unmet need is leadership, maybe negotiate internally before leaving.

4. **What will your calendar look like in each job?**  
   Compare actual weeks, not job-title narratives.

5. **Which option gives better two-year optionality?**  
   Staying in frontier research may preserve one type of option value; getting formal management experience may create another.

6. **Would you regret leaving the frontier of work you find uniquely meaningful?**  
   Regret/identity can matter more than day-to-day “happiness.”

So: yes, there is research suggesting that choosing the maximally interesting job does not reliably make people dramatically happier. Interest fit matters, but usually modestly. If the Google role gives you autonomy, leadership growth, good people, and reasonable stress, it could plausibly make you as happy or happier despite being less intellectually exciting. But if Anthropic gives you rare meaning/flow/learning that you would strongly miss, that is a real cost too.

answer:
Yes — the research is much more nuanced than “choose the most interesting work and you’ll be happiest.”

The short version: **interestingness/person–job fit matters, but it is not usually the dominant predictor of job happiness once the job is already good.** Other things — autonomy, quality of manager/team, perceived impact, competence/growth, stress, social support, fairness, and work-life fit — are often as important or more important. So the evidence does *not* strongly support “pick the maximally intellectually interesting job” as a universal rule.

## What the research roughly says

### 1. Interest fit predicts job satisfaction, but modestly

There is a long vocational-psychology literature on “person–environment fit,” especially Holland-style interest congruence: do your interests match the work environment?

Meta-analyses generally find that interest congruence is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is usually **small to moderate**, often around correlation levels of roughly **0.1–0.3**, depending on measurement and population. See, e.g., Assouline & Meir 1987; Spokane, Meir & Catalano 2000; Nye et al. 2012.

That is real, but it is not “this determines your happiness.”

Also, much of the evidence is correlational. People who are generally happier, more agentic, or better at finding good environments may report both higher fit and higher satisfaction.

### 2. Broader “person–job fit” matters more than mere intellectual interest

When fit is measured more broadly — abilities fit the job, needs are met by the job, values align with the organization — the relationship with job satisfaction is stronger. Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005 find sizeable associations between perceived fit and job satisfaction.

But this is broader than “is the topic fascinating?” It includes things like:

- Do I get to use my strengths?
- Do I have autonomy?
- Do I respect the mission?
- Do I like the people?
- Does the job give me the kind of responsibility I want?
- Do I feel competent and useful?

Your Google offer may score lower on “technical/research interestingness” but higher on “leadership fit,” “growth,” “status,” “autonomy,” or “impact through others.”

### 3. Job design variables are very important

The job-characteristics literature finds strong links between satisfaction and things like:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task significance,
- feedback,
- social support,
- role clarity,
- manageable demands.

A major meta-analysis by Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007 found that motivational and social characteristics of jobs are robustly related to satisfaction and motivation.

This is important because “interesting research” is only one route to engagement. Leadership can also be engaging if it gives you autonomy, social connection, responsibility, mentoring, and visible impact. Conversely, a technically fascinating IC role can still become unsatisfying if you feel blocked, under-recognized, isolated, or unable to grow.

### 4. Salary has surprisingly weak effects once you are already well paid

There is a meta-analysis by Judge et al. 2010 suggesting that pay level is only weakly related to job satisfaction. Income does matter for life satisfaction and security, but if both jobs are already high-paying and the Google offer is only “a bit higher,” the marginal happiness gain from salary alone is probably small unless it materially changes your financial goals, family plans, risk tolerance, or location options.

### 5. People often overpredict how much one feature will affect happiness

Affective forecasting research — Wilson & Gilbert, among others — shows that people often overestimate the enduring effect of salient life changes. In job decisions, one salient difference like “this work is more interesting” or “this role gives me leadership” can dominate your imagination, while mundane daily realities matter a lot:

- Who is my manager?
- How many hours of meetings?
- How much bureaucracy?
- How much control over roadmap?
- Do I like the team?
- Am I proud of the product?
- Do I feel momentum?
- Am I learning?

So you are right to be skeptical of advice that overweights “interestingness” in the abstract.

## The key distinction: “interesting enough” vs “maximally interesting”

The evidence seems more consistent with:

> Try to find work that is engaging, meaningful, and well-fit enough, but do not optimize only for intellectual interestingness.

There may be diminishing returns. If both jobs clear some threshold of “not boring / not soul-deadening,” then differences in autonomy, team quality, leadership growth, stress, and meaning may dominate.

But if the Google job is not merely “less interesting” but actually boring, politically frustrating, or misaligned with your values, that is different.

## In your case

You have two major pulls:

### Anthropic
Pros:
- Highly interesting work.
- Likely strong mission/field relevance.
- Frontier research environment.
- Probably better intellectual fit.

Possible cons:
- Less direct leadership opportunity.
- Maybe slower path to people management.
- Possible frustration if you want leverage through others.

### Google
Pros:
- Slightly higher salary.
- Small-team leadership, which you have “always wanted.”
- Management/leadership skill-building.
- Brand/infrastructure/resources.
- Potentially broader career option value.

Possible cons:
- Less interesting work.
- More bureaucracy.
- Possible lower mission alignment or urgency.
- “Leading a small team” may involve less real authority than advertised.

The crucial question is not “interesting vs money.” The crucial question is:

> Would the leadership component at Google be intrinsically motivating enough to compensate for lower research interest?

For some people, absolutely yes. Leading, mentoring, setting direction, and being responsible for outcomes can be deeply satisfying. For others, management turns into calendar Tetris, performance reviews, politics, and loss of craft.

## I would investigate these before deciding

Ask Google very concretely:

1. **What fraction of my time will be technical work vs meetings/process?**
2. **Will I have real authority over roadmap, hiring, evaluation, priorities?**
3. **Am I a people manager, tech lead, or project lead?**
4. **What does success look like after 6 and 12 months?**
5. **Why did the previous person leave or why was the role created?**
6. **What are the team’s biggest frustrations?**
7. **How much freedom does the team have vs directives from above?**
8. **Can I talk to peers who recently joined similar roles?**
9. **If I dislike management, can I move back to a strong IC/research role internally?**

Ask Anthropic, perhaps delicately:

1. **Is there a path for me to lead a small team/project here?**
2. **Could I manage/mentor 1–3 people or become a tech lead?**
3. **What would I need to demonstrate to get that opportunity?**
4. **Are there upcoming projects where I could take more ownership?**

It may be that the best option is not “Anthropic IC vs Google lead,” but “Anthropic with a negotiated leadership trajectory.”

## My read of the evidence

If you are asking, “Does research prove that picking the most interesting job makes people much happier?” — **no.**

A more evidence-aligned view is:

- Avoid jobs that are boring, aversive, or value-misaligned.
- Prefer jobs with autonomy, competence growth, good people, manageable stress, and meaning.
- Intellectual interest matters, especially for someone like you, but it is not the only or necessarily biggest input.
- A leadership role can be more satisfying than a more interesting IC role if it matches your desired identity and gives real agency.
- But do not take “leadership” at face value; verify the day-to-day reality.

My practical bias: **If the Google role is genuinely a chance to lead a good team with real autonomy, and the work is still at least moderately interesting, it could be rational to take it.** But if “less interesting” actually means “I won’t care about the work,” or if the leadership is mostly bureaucracy without real ownership, staying at Anthropic — or negotiating leadership there — seems better.

answer:
Yes. The evidence is broadly: **interest fit matters, but it is not usually the dominant predictor of happiness/job satisfaction**, and “choose the most interesting possible work” is probably too strong as career advice.

A few relevant findings:

1. **Interest–job fit has a real but modest relationship with satisfaction.**  
   Vocational psychology studies often use Holland/RIASEC-style “interest congruence” — whether someone’s interests match their occupation. Meta-analyses generally find positive but not huge correlations, often around **r ≈ .15–.25** with job satisfaction. That means interest match explains only a small-to-moderate fraction of the variance. It helps, but it is far from the whole story.

2. **Broader person–job fit matters more than “interestingness.”**  
   Meta-analyses of person–job fit find stronger relationships with job satisfaction, sometimes around **r ≈ .5**, but this includes many things besides intellectual interest: skill fit, autonomy, values, workload, compensation, status, ability to succeed, etc. So “I find the topic fascinating” is only one component of fit.

3. **Work design variables are very important.**  
   Research on job characteristics finds that things like **autonomy, feedback, task significance, skill variety, social support, and manageable demands** are strongly related to satisfaction and motivation. A less intellectually exciting job with more autonomy, leadership, ownership, mentoring, and impact can sometimes be more satisfying than a more fascinating IC role with less agency.

4. **Salary has a positive but usually modest effect, especially at high income levels.**  
   Pay matters, but meta-analyses tend to find only small-to-moderate correlations between salary and job satisfaction. A “bit higher salary” probably should not dominate the decision unless it meaningfully changes your financial security or life options.

5. **People are bad at forecasting what will make them happy.**  
   Affective forecasting research suggests people overweight salient differences — prestige, topic interestingness, salary, title — and underweight day-to-day experience: meetings, manager quality, autonomy, colleagues, stress, commute, sleep, and whether the work gives them a sense of progress.

So the research-supported answer is something like:

> Don’t maximize “most interesting possible topic.” Instead, choose work that is interesting enough, with high autonomy, good colleagues, meaningful responsibility, sustainable demands, and strong long-term fit.

For your specific case, the key question may be whether **leading a small team** is merely a title you want, or whether the actual daily work of leading — planning, unblocking, coaching, hiring, resolving ambiguity, absorbing organizational stress — is itself interesting to you. If yes, the Google role may be more interesting in a different dimension. If no, you may be trading unusually stimulating research for a less exciting job plus a management label.

I’d ask yourself:

- Will I enjoy the **weekly calendar** of the Google job?
- Is the team-lead role real authority or mostly coordination?
- Would I be learning a skill I deeply want — management, strategy, scaling research?
- Is the Google work “less interesting” or actually “boring/low-mission”?
- Can I get a team-lead path at Anthropic within a reasonable timeframe?
- If I imagine taking the Google role, what would make me regret it after 12 months?
- If I imagine staying at Anthropic, what would make me regret not taking the opportunity?

My read: if Anthropic is giving you rare intellectual energy and mission alignment, a small salary bump alone is probably not enough reason to leave. But if managing/leading is a central career goal and the Google role gives you a credible path into that, it could be worth taking even if the technical topic is less exciting. The research says interest matters — just not so much that it should crowd out autonomy, growth, people, leadership, and day-to-day fit.

answer:
Yes — the evidence is broadly consistent with: **“interesting work matters, but it is not usually the dominant determinant of happiness, and people often overweight it when imagining jobs.”** The strongest version of “always pick the most interesting work” is not well-supported.

### What the research tends to find

#### 1. Interest–job fit predicts satisfaction, but only modestly
The closest literature is on **vocational interest congruence**: whether your work matches your interests/personality, e.g. Holland-code fit. Meta-analyses generally find a **real but modest** relationship with job satisfaction — often correlations around **r ≈ .10–.30**, depending on measurement.

Classic examples:

- **Assouline & Meir 1987**, meta-analysis on person–environment congruence and well-being.
- **Tranberg, Slane & Ekeberg 1993**, meta-analysis on interest congruence and satisfaction.
- Later vocational-interest work, e.g. **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow 2012**, finds interests predict performance/persistence too, but again not overwhelmingly.

Interpretation: if your job is a much better interest fit, you are *somewhat* more likely to be satisfied. But most variance in job satisfaction is explained by other things.

#### 2. Broader “person–job fit” matters more than “interestingness”
When researchers look at broader fit — whether the job gives you what you need, uses your abilities, matches your values, provides autonomy, etc. — effects are larger.

For example, **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005** found person–job fit and person–organization fit are strongly associated with job satisfaction and commitment. But this includes many things besides “the topic is intellectually exciting”: autonomy, values, social fit, workload, recognition, growth, etc.

So the question is less:

> “Which job has the most interesting content?”

and more:

> “Which job gives me the best overall fit for my needs over the next 2–4 years?”

#### 3. Autonomy, manager quality, coworkers, and work design are huge
Job-design research — e.g. the **Job Characteristics Model** and later meta-analyses such as **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007** — finds that things like:

- autonomy,
- task significance,
- feedback,
- variety,
- role clarity,
- social support,
- good management,

are very strong predictors of satisfaction.

This is relevant because “leading a small team” may increase autonomy, influence, relatedness, and growth — all of which can be deeply satisfying even if the object-level technical work is less thrilling.

But it cuts both ways: if the Google role is mostly meetings, process, consensus-seeking, and low-agency execution, then “team lead” may sound better than it feels.

#### 4. People are bad at affective forecasting
There is a large literature on **affective forecasting** — people often mispredict what will make them happy. See work by **Gilbert, Wilson, Kahneman, Schkade**, etc.

The relevant lesson: when evaluating a life change, people overweight the most salient difference — here, “interesting Anthropic research” vs. “less interesting Google work” — and underweight repeated daily experiences: boss, meetings, pace, commute, conflict, autonomy, team quality, bureaucracy, sleep, exercise, etc.

So yes, there is reason to doubt that maximizing “interestingness” maximizes lived happiness.

#### 5. Compensation helps, but also not as much as people expect
Pay is correlated with satisfaction, but not hugely once basic needs/security are handled. **Judge et al. 2010** meta-analysis found pay level has only a modest association with job satisfaction.

That said, in your case “a bit higher salary” probably matters less as hedonic pleasure and more as **option value**: savings, runway, flexibility, family/security goals, ability to take risks later.

### Applying this to your situation

I would not frame it as:

> Interesting Anthropic job vs. boring Google job.

I’d frame it as:

> Current role: high intellectual interest, likely strong mission/context fit.  
> Google role: management experiment, higher compensation, maybe more leadership growth, but lower intellectual pull.

The key question is whether **management/leadership is itself an intrinsically interesting direction for you**. If leading people, setting direction, mentoring, hiring, and building a team are things you genuinely want, then the Google role may not be “less interesting” — it may be interesting on a different axis.

But if the real picture is:

> “I’ll have a title, but little strategic control, lots of coordination, less frontier work, and a less motivating mission,”

then I would be cautious.

### A practical decision test

Ask yourself:

1. **Typical Tuesday test**  
   In each role, what does a normal Tuesday look like six months from now? Which one do you want to live?

2. **Energy test**  
   Which job would you voluntarily read/think/talk about outside work? Which one would drain you?

3. **Growth test**  
   Which role makes you more valuable three years from now? Frontier research depth? Management track? Both?

4. **Regret test**  
   If you stay at Anthropic, will you regret not trying leadership?  
   If you go to Google, will you regret leaving uniquely interesting work during an unusually important period?

5. **Reversibility test**  
   Could you return to frontier AI research after 1–2 years at Google? Probably, but maybe with some loss of momentum/network/context.  
   Could you get leadership opportunities at Anthropic if you stay and explicitly ask? Maybe worth exploring.

6. **Dominated-option test**  
   Before deciding, ask Anthropic whether there is a path to leading a small team or taking more ownership. If you can get “interesting work + leadership growth,” that may dominate.

### My read from the literature

The evidence does **not** support “always choose the most interesting job.” It supports something more like:

> Choose a job with strong overall fit: enough interest to stay engaged, enough autonomy to feel agency, good people, good manager, sustainable lifestyle, fair compensation, and a growth path you actually want.

For you, the crux is probably not salary. It is:

> Is “leading a small team at Google” a genuine step toward the kind of person/career you want, or is it a shiny title attached to less alive work?

If it is real leadership, with autonomy and a good manager, it could be worth taking even if the research is less exciting. If the leadership is shallow or the work feels meaningfully less motivating, staying at Anthropic — or negotiating for leadership there — may be better.

answer:
Short answer: **yes, there is evidence that “interest fit” matters, but it is not usually the whole story, and the effect is often smaller than people intuitively expect.** The strongest takeaway from the literature is not “do the most interesting possible work.” It is more like: **choose work with good overall fit — autonomy, competence/growth, social environment, values, workload, manager quality, and enough intrinsic interest.**

For your Anthropic vs Google choice, that means: the Google role being “less interesting” is a real cost, but it may be outweighed if the leadership opportunity gives you autonomy, learning, status, mentorship, and a path you deeply want. Conversely, if “less interesting” actually means “I will be bored, politically constrained, and far from the frontier,” that cost could be large.

### What the research roughly says

#### 1. Interest/job congruence predicts job satisfaction, but modestly

Vocational psychology has studied “person–environment fit” or “interest congruence”: e.g. whether someone with investigative/scientific interests ends up in investigative/scientific work. Meta-analyses generally find **positive but modest correlations** between interest congruence and job satisfaction — often around the `r ≈ .10–.25` range depending on method and sample.

That means interest fit matters, but it usually explains only a small-to-moderate fraction of variance in satisfaction. It is not “pick the maximally interesting domain and happiness follows.”

Related work on broader **person–job fit** finds larger effects, but that broader construct includes not only interest, but also skill fit, values, rewards, autonomy, workload, and needs being met. So “fit” matters a lot; “intellectual interestingness” alone is only one part of fit.

#### 2. “Interesting work” overlaps with autonomy, skill variety, challenge, and meaning

The job characteristics literature — Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model and later meta-analyses — finds that jobs tend to be more satisfying when they have:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task significance,
- task identity,
- feedback,
- social support,
- reasonable workload.

“Interesting work” often bundles several of these together. Frontier research is interesting partly because it has novelty, autonomy, high skill use, and perceived significance. If the Google role has lower novelty but higher autonomy/leadership/impact, it may still score well. If it has lower novelty and lower autonomy, that is much worse.

#### 3. People often overpredict the happiness effect of salient differences

Affective forecasting research — e.g. work by Daniel Gilbert, Tim Wilson, Kahneman, Schkade — suggests people often overweight the feature currently in focus. When comparing jobs, the salient difference might be “the work is more/less interesting,” “the salary is higher,” or “I get to lead a team.” But after adaptation, day-to-day happiness often depends more on repeated daily experiences: meetings, manager, team quality, sense of progress, interruptions, sleep, commute, autonomy, bureaucracy, and whether you feel respected.

So if you are currently focusing on “interestingness,” you may overestimate its long-run effect. But likewise, you may also overestimate “leading a team” or “higher salary.”

#### 4. Job satisfaction and life satisfaction are related, but not identical

Meta-analyses typically find a moderate relationship between job satisfaction and life satisfaction, often around `r ≈ .3–.5`. Work matters, especially for people who identify strongly with their work, but it is not all of life satisfaction. At your likely compensation level, marginal salary increases probably have diminishing returns unless the money materially changes your freedom, security, or family situation.

#### 5. Intrinsic motivation matters more for high-skill creative work

For research/engineering roles, intrinsic motivation can be especially important. Teresa Amabile’s work on creativity and intrinsic motivation, as well as self-determination theory from Deci and Ryan, supports the idea that autonomy, competence, and intrinsic interest are important for creative performance and motivation.

So the correct conclusion is not “interestingness does not matter.” It is: **interest matters, but mostly as part of a larger motivational system.** If the Google role removes your sense of curiosity, agency, and frontier relevance, that could hurt both happiness and performance.

### My read on your specific tradeoff

You are choosing between:

**Anthropic**
- very interesting work,
- likely high mission/technical frontier alignment,
- maybe less immediate people-leadership opportunity,
- current known environment.

**Google**
- somewhat higher salary,
- leading a small team,
- something you have wanted,
- less interesting work,
- likely more organizational complexity/bureaucracy.

The key question is whether the Google role is “less interesting but still engaging” or “less interesting in a way that will make me feel I left the frontier.”

Also, “leading a small team” may itself become the interesting thing. Management/tech leadership can be intellectually rich: setting direction, growing people, prioritizing under uncertainty, aligning stakeholders, designing team culture. But if what you truly want is research taste, direct technical work, and proximity to frontier AI, management of a less exciting product area may not scratch that itch.

### A useful decision frame

I would score both roles from 1–10 on these dimensions:

1. **Intellectual interest**  
2. **Autonomy/control over direction**  
3. **Learning rate**  
4. **Quality of manager and peers**  
5. **Mission/value alignment**  
6. **Leadership growth**  
7. **Career capital / future optionality**  
8. **Work-life sustainability**  
9. **Compensation / financial freedom**  
10. **Reversibility** — can you get back to frontier research if you dislike it?

Then ask: which role has the better expected 2-year outcome?

In your case, I would especially investigate:

- Can you get a small-team leadership path at Anthropic if you ask?
- At Google, how much real authority would you have?
- Would you still be close to technical decision-making?
- Is the team/project strategically important or a backwater?
- Who is your manager, and do they have a track record of growing leads?
- If you dislike it after 12–18 months, can you transfer internally or return to research-heavy roles?
- Are you excited by the day-to-day calendar of the Google role, not just the title?

### Bottom line

The research does support skepticism toward “just pick the most interesting job.” Interest fit predicts satisfaction, but not overwhelmingly; autonomy, social environment, values, manager quality, growth, and life constraints also matter a lot.

But in frontier AI research, intrinsic interest may be unusually valuable. So I would not discount it too much. The right move depends on whether the Google role gives you a new form of intrinsic motivation — leadership, ownership, mentoring, influence — or merely trades away rare intellectual excitement for title and salary.

answer:
Short answer: **there isn’t strong evidence that “pick the most interesting job possible” is the happiness-maximizing rule.** There *is* strong evidence that interesting/meaningful/autonomous work matters. But the research is more consistent with: **work happiness comes from a bundle of factors**—autonomy, competence/growth, social environment, manager, meaning, stress, hours, commute, compensation/security, and fit—not from “interestingness” alone.

A few relevant findings/literatures:

1. **Interest fit predicts satisfaction, but not overwhelmingly.**  
   Vocational-interest/person-job fit studies generally find that fit with one’s interests is associated with job satisfaction, but effect sizes are often modest. Broader “person-job fit” matters more, but that includes many things beyond topic interest: skills, values, needs, work style, etc.

2. **Job design matters a lot.**  
   The Job Characteristics Model and later meta-analyses find that satisfaction is strongly related to things like **autonomy, skill variety, feedback, task significance, and social support**. So a “less fascinating” role with more autonomy, leadership, impact, and good colleagues can beat a more intellectually exciting role with less control or worse day-to-day conditions.

3. **Meaning and autonomy are often more robust than novelty/interestingness.**  
   Self-determination theory research finds that well-being at work is strongly tied to **autonomy, competence, and relatedness**. A team-lead role could score high here if you genuinely enjoy mentoring, setting direction, and owning outcomes.

4. **People are bad at affective forecasting.**  
   Research by Kahneman, Gilbert, Wilson, and others on focusing illusions/affective forecasting suggests people often overestimate how much one salient feature will affect their happiness. In career decisions, “the work is more interesting,” “the salary is higher,” or “I’ll be a manager” can each become a focal point while the real happiness drivers are mundane: calendars, meetings, manager quality, stress, autonomy, commute, and whether you like your coworkers.

5. **Salary increases have diminishing returns, especially at high incomes.**  
   Pay matters, but meta-analyses generally find the relationship between pay and job satisfaction is weaker than people expect once basic comfort/security are met. So “a bit higher salary” probably should not dominate unless the difference materially changes your life or financial freedom.

So the slogan “do the most interesting work possible” is too simple. A better version might be:

> Choose the role with the best expected day-to-day experience and long-term trajectory, where “interestingness” is one major input but not the only one.

For your specific case, I’d separate the offer into components:

- **Anthropic:** very interesting work, probably strong mission/technical frontier, maybe high intellectual engagement.
- **Google:** higher salary, leadership experience, maybe broader organizational leverage, but less interesting technical content.

The key question is whether “less interesting” means:

1. **Still pretty interesting, just not maximally frontier-level** — in which case Google could be great, especially if leadership is a real goal; or  
2. **Actively dull / misaligned / low-energy for you** — in which case the leadership title and salary may not compensate.

I’d also ask Google very concrete questions:

- What would my calendar look like in a typical week?
- How much time is technical vs people management vs planning/process?
- What decisions would I actually own?
- What does success look like after 6 and 12 months?
- Who is my manager, and how much autonomy would I have?
- If I dislike management, can I return to a senior IC path?
- Is the team doing work I can become interested in, or is it intrinsically not my thing?

And ask yourself:

- If I took Google and after 18 months had become a much better leader but was less excited technically, would I be glad?
- If I stayed at Anthropic and remained highly engaged technically but did not get leadership soon, would I regret it?
- Is leading a team something I want in reality, or something I want as an identity/status marker?
- Can I get a leadership path at Anthropic if I explicitly ask for it?

My read from the research: **don’t blindly maximize interestingness**, but also don’t dismiss it. For a research engineer at the frontier, intellectual interest may be unusually central to motivation and long-run performance. If the Google role is only moderately less interesting and gives you real autonomy/leadership growth, it could plausibly make you equally or more satisfied. If it feels like a clear step away from what gives you energy, the salary/title bump may fade quickly.

answer:
Yes—there is evidence in both directions, but the short version is:

**“Interesting work” matters, but the evidence does not support a simple rule like “choose the most interesting job and you’ll be much happier.”** Interest fit tends to have **real but modest** associations with job satisfaction. Other factors—autonomy, competence growth, social environment, stress, mission/meaning, manager quality, and daily work texture—often matter as much or more.

### What the research roughly says

1. **Interest/job fit predicts satisfaction, but not overwhelmingly.**  
   Meta-analyses on vocational interest congruence—e.g. Holland/RIASEC-style “does this job match what you like?”—usually find correlations with job satisfaction around **r ≈ .20–.30**. That is meaningful, but it explains only a minority of the variance.  
   Interpretation: people are usually happier when their work fits their interests, but “most interesting possible” is not a guaranteed happiness maximizer.

2. **Job design factors often predict satisfaction more strongly.**  
   Work-design research, e.g. Hackman & Oldham and later meta-analyses such as Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007, finds that factors like:
   - autonomy,
   - skill variety,
   - task significance,
   - feedback,
   - problem-solving/complexity,
   - social support  
   
   are pretty strongly associated with job satisfaction and motivation. Some of these overlap with “interesting,” but not perfectly. A job can be intellectually fascinating but low-autonomy, chaotic, politically frustrating, or socially draining.

3. **People are not great at forecasting what will make them happy.**  
   Affective-forecasting research—Wilson & Gilbert, Kahneman’s “focusing illusion,” etc.—suggests people often overestimate the long-term emotional impact of one salient factor. In a career decision, “the work is more interesting” or “I’d finally manage people” can become the focus, while day-to-day realities—meetings, manager, pace, ambiguity, commute, status anxiety, ability to do deep work—drive actual happiness.

4. **At already-high income levels, somewhat higher salary probably has limited happiness impact.**  
   Income does correlate with well-being, but mostly logarithmically/diminishingly. If both offers are already financially excellent, “a bit higher salary” may matter less than role quality, unless it materially changes your financial freedom or family situation.

5. **Leadership can be a real source of satisfaction—but it changes the job.**  
   If leading a small team is something you have always wanted, that is not a trivial consideration. Management can increase autonomy, impact, status, mentoring satisfaction, and career option value. But it often reduces time spent on deep technical/research work and increases time spent on coordination, performance management, org politics, hiring, and ambiguity.

### Applying this to your Anthropic vs Google choice

I would not frame it as:

> “Interesting research vs less interesting job.”

I’d frame it as:

> “Which role gives me the better two-year life: growth, autonomy, meaning, people, energy, and future optionality?”

Some questions I’d ask yourself:

- **Do I want to become a manager, or do I want to have managed?**  
  The second is common. The first means enjoying 1:1s, delegation, conflict resolution, project shaping, hiring, feedback, prioritization, and being responsible for other people’s growth.

- **Is the Google role less interesting in topic, but more interesting in craft?**  
  Leading a team can be intellectually interesting in a different way: strategy, systems, people, leverage, execution.

- **Is Anthropic interesting because of the work itself, the mission, the coworkers, or the current historical moment?**  
  If the “interesting” part is deeply tied to your values and identity, discounting it may be a mistake.

- **Could you get the leadership path at Anthropic within a reasonable time?**  
  If yes, the Google offer may be useful leverage/information rather than a necessary move.

- **What would a typical Tuesday look like in each role 12 months from now?**  
  Not the pitch, not the peak moments—the modal week. How many hours of deep work? How many meetings? How much agency? How much energy at the end of the day?

### My read from the research

The evidence supports this position:

> Do not maximize “interestingness” alone. But also do not dismiss it, especially if you are a research-oriented person whose motivation comes strongly from intellectual engagement and mission.

For someone already at a very high compensation level, I would personally weight:

1. quality of daily work,
2. autonomy,
3. people/manager,
4. mission/meaning,
5. growth trajectory,
6. option value,
7. compensation delta.

If Google gives you a credible management path you strongly want, with good people and sane scope, it could be worth taking even if the technical topic is less exciting. But if “less interesting” means “I will feel detached from the mission and spend most of my week in coordination I don’t enjoy,” the higher salary and title may not compensate.

A useful practical move: ask Google for very concrete details—expected calendar, decision rights, team quality, roadmap ownership, amount of IC/research time, performance expectations. And ask Anthropic whether there is a plausible path to leading people or a small project/team. The best outcome may be discovering that you can get more leadership without leaving the more meaningful work.

answer:
Short answer: **yes, there is research supporting skepticism about “pick the most interesting work possible” as a happiness-maximization rule**. But the evidence does **not** say interesting work is unimportant. It says that “interestingness” is only one component of work well-being, and people often overfocus on salient job attributes while underweighting day-to-day factors like autonomy, manager/team quality, stress, status/control, learning, and fit with long-term identity.

## What the research roughly says

### 1. Intrinsic interest matters, but it is not everything
Classic work-design research — e.g. Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model and later meta-analyses like Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007 — finds that jobs are more satisfying when they have:

- autonomy  
- skill variety  
- task significance  
- feedback  
- task identity  
- opportunities for learning/problem-solving  

So if “interesting” means “I get to solve hard problems, learn, have autonomy, and do meaningful work,” then yes, that is quite predictive of job satisfaction.

But if “interesting” means specifically “the research topic is intellectually fascinating,” the evidence is less decisive. Topic-interest is only one part of the broader work design.

### 2. Interest/job-congruence effects are usually modest
Vocational psychology studies on person–job fit and interest congruence — e.g. Holland-type fit research, Spokane/Meir/Catalano 2000, Kristof-Brown/Zimmerman/Johnson 2005 — generally find that fit with one’s interests predicts satisfaction, but the effect is usually **small-to-moderate**, not overwhelming.

In other words: people whose jobs match their interests are, on average, more satisfied — but not so much that interest dominates everything else.

A role with somewhat less intellectually exciting content but much more autonomy, better relationships, more ownership, and a desired leadership path can easily beat a more fascinating individual-contributor role on overall well-being.

### 3. People often mispredict what will make them happy at work
Affective forecasting research — Wilson & Gilbert, Kahneman, etc. — suggests people overestimate how much single salient factors will affect their future happiness. In career choices, one may overfocus on:

- prestige  
- salary  
- “interestingness” of the domain  
- company brand  
- being close to frontier work  

while underweighting:

- how good/bad a typical Tuesday feels  
- whether you respect your manager  
- decision-making speed  
- psychological safety  
- meeting load  
- interruptions  
- whether you feel ownership  
- whether you are growing in the direction you want  

A directly relevant study is Iyengar, Wells & Schwartz 2006, “Doing Better but Feeling Worse”: people who maximized during job search got objectively better outcomes, including higher salaries, but were less satisfied with the outcome. This is not specifically about “most interesting job,” but it is evidence against the general strategy of optimizing too hard for an idealized best option.

### 4. Salary increases matter less at high income levels
Compensation is not irrelevant, especially if it changes your savings rate, family security, donation capacity, or independence. But for someone already in a high-paying research-engineering role, the marginal happiness effect of “a bit higher salary” is probably smaller than the effect of daily work quality, autonomy, stress, and long-term career fit.

### 5. Leadership may be its own source of “interestingness”
You describe the Google role as “much less interesting,” but also as “leading a small team,” which you have always wanted to do. That matters.

Management/leadership can satisfy different psychological needs:

- autonomy: shaping direction  
- competence: learning a new craft  
- relatedness: mentoring, helping people grow  
- significance: multiplying impact through others  
- identity: becoming the kind of person/leader you want to be  

So the comparison may not be:

> interesting work vs boring work

but rather:

> intellectual/research interest vs leadership/growth/ownership interest

Those are genuinely different sources of motivation.

## How I would interpret the evidence for your case

The research does **not** support a simple rule like:

> Always choose the most interesting technical work.

A better rule is:

> Choose the role whose average week and 2-year trajectory best satisfy autonomy, competence/growth, relatedness, meaning, manageable stress, and future optionality.

For you, the core question may be:

**Is the Google role less interesting only in topic, or less satisfying in the actual day-to-day?**

If it is less interesting in topic but gives you real leadership, ownership, good people, and a path you deeply want, it could plausibly make you as happy or happier.

If it is less interesting *and* comes with big-company politics, slower execution, less mission fit, shallow leadership authority, and less technical depth, then the higher salary and title may not compensate.

## Practical decision test

I’d compare the roles on a “typical month” basis, not an abstract prestige/interest basis.

Rate each role 1–10 on:

| Dimension | Anthropic | Google |
|---|---:|---:|
| Intellectual excitement |  |  |
| Mission/meaning |  |  |
| Autonomy |  |  |
| Quality of manager/org |  |  |
| People you work with daily |  |  |
| Learning/growth |  |  |
| Leadership development |  |  |
| Stress/sustainability |  |  |
| Future optionality |  |  |
| Compensation/security |  |  |
| Pride/regret if you stay 2 years |  |  |

Then ask two premortem questions:

1. **If I take Google and regret it after 12 months, why?**  
   Possible answers: boring domain, bureaucracy, fake leadership, loss of frontier exposure, too many meetings, less mission fit.

2. **If I stay at Anthropic and regret it after 12 months, why?**  
   Possible answers: missed leadership opportunity, stagnation as IC, lower comp, less ownership, burnout, no management path.

The more concrete regret story is often informative.

## One move I’d strongly consider

Before deciding, try to make the choice less binary.

Ask Anthropic something like:

> “I’m very excited about the work here, but I’m increasingly motivated by technical leadership/people leadership. Is there a plausible path over the next 6–12 months toward leading a small team or owning a larger direction?”

And ask Google:

> “How real is the leadership component? What decisions would I own? How many people? What is the charter? How much technical depth would remain? What would success look like after a year?”

If Anthropic can give you leadership growth, staying becomes more attractive. If Google’s leadership is real and the team is strong, the “less interesting” concern may matter less.

## Bottom line

There is no clean result saying “people are not happier when they choose the most interesting job.” But there is substantial evidence that **interest alone is an incomplete predictor of job happiness**, and that people often overestimate the importance of the most salient job attribute.

So it would be completely reasonable, research-wise, to choose the less intellectually fascinating job if it gives you leadership, autonomy, growth, and a better long-term arc.

But if the Anthropic work is unusually meaningful and energizing in a way you rarely expect to find elsewhere, that is also a real factor — not just romantic career advice.

On the flip side, if daily tasks feel dull with too much bureaucracy, it might lessen happiness. Leading a small team can offer valuable work, but it does take time away from deep technical engagement.
**Developing a decision-making approach**

I'm thinking about a practical decision algorithm to evaluate job offers. I could translate elements into a weekly expectation based on coding, meetings, and management tasks. I’d score dimensions like intrinsic content, autonomy, and compensation. Then it's essential to ask questions like, “Would I regret this in 18 months?” and consider the energy I feel after a typical day. When negotiating, I’d focus on aspects like team structure and autonomy at specific companies. It might also help to try a reversible experiment to reassess in 6-12 months while maintaining my network.
**Investigating job choice and happiness**

I need to explore if people are really happier when they choose the most interesting job. Direct randomized evidence for this idea is nearly nonexistent, which makes measuring happiness tricky. Some meta-analyses on Holland congruence show modest links between job interest and happiness but not as the leading factor. Also, while job satisfaction impacts life satisfaction, it doesn’t account for everything, with a spillover effect around r  0.4. So, it seems the "happy life" extends beyond just job choice.
**Evaluating interest and meaning**

I need to think about including the idea of "hedonic vs eudaimonic" in my work. This concept suggests that interesting work can offer meaning beyond just momentary happiness. While Google might support growth in status or management, that doesn’t always mean daily happiness is guaranteed. I want to finalize by stating: “Short answer: yes, evidence exists against pursuing the 'most interesting topic' mindset, but it doesn’t mean that interest isn’t important.” I also need to cite accurately without using footnotes.
answer:
Short answer: **yes, there’s evidence against “maximize interestingness at all costs,” but not evidence that interesting work is unimportant.** The research picture is more like: people are happier when their work fits them, gives autonomy/mastery/relatedness/meaning, has good colleagues/managers, and doesn’t chronically drain them. “The topic is maximally interesting” is only one part of that.

## What the research roughly says

### 1. Interest/person–job fit matters, but it’s not everything
Studies of vocational interest congruence — e.g. “people with investigative interests in investigative jobs” — generally find **positive but modest-to-moderate relationships** with job satisfaction. Classic/meta-analytic work around Holland fit, e.g. Assouline & Meir 1987; Spokane, Meir & Catalano 2000, tends to find correlations around the “meaningful but not dominant” range.

Broader **person–job fit** is stronger. Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005 meta-analysis found person–job fit was quite strongly related to job satisfaction, but that includes more than interest: skills, values, needs, work style, etc.

So: “interesting domain” matters, but “this whole role fits my motives and day-to-day life” matters more.

### 2. Work design often predicts satisfaction as much as the topic does
The job-characteristics literature — Hackman & Oldham; Fried & Ferris 1987; Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007 — repeatedly finds that people like jobs more when they have:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- feedback,
- task significance,
- social support,
- manageable workload,
- clear ownership.

This is very relevant to your case. A Google role that is “less interesting” technically but gives you **ownership, mentorship, scope, autonomy, and team-building** could be satisfying. Conversely, if it’s “leadership” in name but mostly stakeholder management, perf processes, and alignment meetings, the title may not compensate for lost intrinsic interest.

### 3. Salary helps, but probably less at your level
Pay is related to satisfaction, but the relationship is usually weaker than people expect. Judge et al. 2010 meta-analysis found pay level had only a weak relationship with job satisfaction. On life satisfaction, income does matter, but with diminishing returns and big individual differences; Kahneman & Deaton 2010 and later Killingsworth/Kahneman work complicate the exact “plateau” story.

For someone already highly compensated in frontier AI, **a modest salary bump probably shouldn’t dominate** unless it meaningfully changes your financial freedom, family situation, immigration/security, ability to take risks later, etc.

### 4. People mispredict what will make them happy
Affective-forecasting research by Wilson & Gilbert, plus Kahneman’s “focusing illusion,” suggests people often overweight the salient difference in front of them. In your case, you might overweight:

- “Anthropic work is more interesting,” or
- “Google pays more and lets me lead.”

But your actual happiness may be driven by more mundane variables: your manager, team quality, meeting load, bureaucracy, sleep, commute/remote setup, stress, perceived impact, and how much control you have over your calendar.

### 5. “Follow your passion” is too simple
There’s research suggesting that treating passion as something you must “find” can be misleading. O’Keefe, Dweck & Walton 2018 argue for a “develop your passion” model: interests grow with competence, autonomy, and investment. So a role that starts less intellectually exciting may become meaningful if it gives you a new growth axis — e.g. leadership.

But the reverse is also true: if your deep motivation is frontier technical research, management may not scratch that itch.

## Applying this to your Anthropic vs Google choice

I’d separate the decision into two questions:

### A. Is Google less interesting because the *domain* is less interesting, or because the *day-to-day work* will be less engaging?

Those are very different.

- “Less interesting domain, but I get to mentor, set direction, build a team, own outcomes” → could be great.
- “Less interesting domain, plus lots of process/bureaucracy/stakeholder meetings” → likely worse.
- “Less interesting now, but high autonomy and path to shape the agenda” → maybe good.
- “Less interesting and low agency” → dangerous.

### B. Is leadership a real intrinsic goal or an imagined status marker?

Leading a small team can be deeply satisfying if you enjoy:

- developing people,
- creating clarity,
- making tradeoffs,
- delegating,
- communicating upward and sideways,
- absorbing ambiguity,
- getting less credit for direct technical output.

But if what you actually love is intense technical problem-solving, management can feel like giving up the best part of the job.

## A practical way to decide

For each role, estimate your actual week:

| Dimension | Anthropic | Google |
|---|---:|---:|
| Deep technical work | ? | ? |
| Meetings/stakeholder alignment | ? | ? |
| Autonomy over direction | ? | ? |
| Learning rate | ? | ? |
| Quality of colleagues | ? | ? |
| Manager/support | ? | ? |
| Mission/value alignment | ? | ? |
| Stress / pace | ? | ? |
| Career capital | ? | ? |
| Reversibility | ? | ? |
| Chance to lead | ? | ? |
| Compensation impact | ? | ? |

Then ask: **which role has the better ordinary Tuesday?** Not the better narrative, not the better title, not the more impressive external story — the better ordinary Tuesday.

## My read from the evidence

The research does **not** support “always choose the most interesting possible job.” It supports something closer to:

> Choose the job with the best overall fit across interest, autonomy, growth, relationships, meaning, and sustainable daily experience.

So I would not leave Anthropic merely for a slightly higher salary. I would consider leaving if the Google role gives you a genuinely important missing dimension — leadership — and if you have evidence the team, autonomy, manager, and scope are excellent.

Before deciding, I’d try hard to answer two questions:

1. **Can Anthropic give you a credible path to leading people or owning a small team?**  
   If yes, staying may dominate.

2. **Can Google make the role more intellectually alive — better scope, research angle, technical ownership, publication/open-source freedom, or clear path to more interesting problems?**  
   If no, be cautious.

In other words: don’t maximize “interesting topic,” but also don’t underestimate how rare it is to already have deeply interesting work. The best reason to move would be: **“I want to grow into leadership, and this specific Google role is a high-quality environment for that.”** Not: **“Slightly more money plus team lead title.”**

answer:
Short answer: **yes, there is research suggesting that “pick the maximally interesting job” is not a reliable happiness-maximizing strategy**. But the stronger conclusion from the literature is not “interesting work doesn’t matter.” It is more like:

> Interesting/meaningful work matters a lot, but it is only one component of job satisfaction; people often overpredict how much a single salient job attribute will affect their happiness; and “interest fit” has only modest-to-moderate correlations with satisfaction compared with broader fit, autonomy, people, stress, and values.

## What the research roughly says

### 1. Interest fit helps, but it is not destiny

Vocational psychology has studied “person–environment fit,” especially Holland-style interest congruence: do your interests match your occupation? Meta-analyses generally find **positive but not huge** relationships between interest congruence and job satisfaction — often around **r ≈ .10–.30**, depending on measure and study.

So: people are usually somewhat happier when their work fits their interests, but the effect is far from overwhelming. Plenty of people in “interesting” jobs are unhappy; plenty in less intrinsically fascinating jobs are satisfied because they have autonomy, good colleagues, status, growth, security, or meaningful responsibility.

Relevant lines of work: Assouline & Meir 1987; Spokane, Meir & Catalano 2000; Nye et al. on vocational interests and fit.

### 2. Broader job design variables often matter more than “interestingness”

Work-design research finds that satisfaction is strongly related to things like:

- autonomy,
- skill use,
- task variety,
- feedback,
- task significance,
- manageable workload,
- low role ambiguity,
- good manager/team environment.

The Hackman–Oldham job characteristics model and later meta-analyses, e.g. Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007, generally support this. “Interestingness” overlaps with skill variety, challenge, and task significance, but it is not identical to them.

In your case, “leading a small team” could score highly on autonomy, responsibility, mentoring, scope, and growth — even if the technical content is less intrinsically exciting.

### 3. People are bad at forecasting how much one job feature will affect happiness

Affective forecasting research — Wilson & Gilbert, Kahneman’s “focusing illusion,” etc. — suggests that when comparing options, we overweight the dimension currently in focus. If you are comparing “Anthropic = fascinating” vs “Google = less fascinating,” your mind may exaggerate how much that one variable will dominate daily life.

But the reverse can also happen: Google’s higher salary/title/team-lead path may loom large now, while the day-to-day boredom may be underweighted.

The key is to imagine the **ordinary Tuesday**, not the headline version of the job.

### 4. Maximizing can make people less satisfied

One especially relevant study is Iyengar, Wells & Schwartz 2006, “Doing Better but Feeling Worse.” They studied graduating students entering the job market and found that “maximizers” — people trying hard to get the absolute best option — obtained better objective outcomes, such as higher salaries, but reported lower satisfaction and more negative affect.

This does not mean you should be careless. But it does suggest that trying to find the globally optimal, most interesting, most prestigious, best-paid, best-career-capital job can backfire. A “satisficing” strategy may be healthier: define minimum thresholds on the important dimensions, then choose among options that clear them.

### 5. Salary matters, but marginally less at high income

The old “happiness plateaus at $75k” result is too simplistic. Newer work, e.g. Killingsworth 2021 and Kahneman/Killingsworth/Mellers 2023, suggests happiness often continues rising with income, roughly logarithmically. But at the kind of compensation levels likely relevant here, a salary increase is usually not the dominant driver of daily happiness unless it materially changes your life goals, security, family options, location, or freedom.

So “a bit higher salary” is nice, but probably should not dominate if the day-to-day work would feel deadening.

## My read for your specific situation

The key distinction is:

### Case A: “Google is less interesting technically, but the leadership problem is genuinely interesting to me.”

Then the offer may be very attractive. Management can be a different kind of intellectual challenge: hiring, coaching, strategy, prioritization, org design, taste formation, conflict resolution, aligning research/product/infra, etc. If you have long wanted to lead a team, this could be valuable career exploration. You may learn whether you actually enjoy management before you are too senior to experiment cheaply.

### Case B: “Google is less interesting, and the only appealing parts are pay/title/status.”

Then I would be cautious. The evidence suggests people adapt to pay/status faster than to the daily structure of their work. If you expect to spend most days on problems you do not care about, with lower mission-fit and less intellectual excitement, that is a real cost.

### Case C: “Anthropic is unusually meaningful/rare, but I’m tempted by Google because leadership is unavailable where I am.”

Then the question becomes: can you get the leadership growth without leaving? For example:

- Can you lead an intern, contractor, small project, or cross-functional effort?
- Can you become tech lead on a subarea?
- Can you explicitly tell your manager that people leadership is a serious goal?
- Is there a path to managing or leading a small team within 6–18 months?

If yes, staying may dominate. If no, Google may be a rational move even if the technical content is less exciting.

## A practical decision rule

I would not ask, “Which job is most interesting?”  
I would ask:

> “Will this job clear my minimum bar for intellectual engagement, while improving other dimensions I care about?”

Score both jobs on something like:

| Dimension | Anthropic | Google |
|---|---:|---:|
| Intellectual interest | ? | ? |
| Mission/meaning | ? | ? |
| Autonomy | ? | ? |
| Quality of manager | ? | ? |
| Quality of peers/team | ? | ? |
| Leadership growth | ? | ? |
| Learning rate | ? | ? |
| Future optionality | ? | ? |
| Compensation/security | ? | ? |
| Lifestyle/stress | ? | ? |
| Prestige/career capital | ? | ? |
| “Ordinary Tuesday” happiness | ? | ? |

The “ordinary Tuesday” row is especially important.

## Questions I would ask Google before deciding

1. What would my calendar look like in a typical week?
2. How much of the role is people management vs technical leadership vs execution?
3. Do I own roadmap, hiring, performance reviews, prioritization?
4. How much autonomy does the team actually have?
5. Why is this team important to Google?
6. What are the hardest unsolved problems the team faces?
7. What would make me bored or frustrated in this role?
8. If I decide management is not for me, can I return to an IC/research path?
9. Is there internal mobility into more technically interesting areas later?
10. What would success after 12 months look like?

## Bottom line

Research does support your suspicion: **people are not simply happiest when they pick the most interesting job possible**. Interest fit matters, but it is not overwhelmingly predictive, and people often overestimate the happiness impact of salient job features.

But I would not downgrade “interesting work” too much. At high skill/high income levels, daily intellectual engagement, autonomy, meaning, and peer quality can matter more than incremental salary.

So my tentative heuristic would be:

- If Google is “less interesting but still interesting enough,” and team leadership is a major life/career goal, the offer is seriously worth considering.
- If Google feels “substantially boring” and the main upside is salary/title, I would be wary.
- If Anthropic is uniquely meaningful and you can plausibly get leadership growth there, staying may be the better compounder.
